data.content:
Campbell
Author of a technique relying on a modified Abel lemma to obtain a simplified proof.

Cantarini
Co-author of a technique relying on a modified Abel lemma to obtain a simplified proof.

Yamaguchi
Author of a q-analogue whose simplified proof is presented with a new q-analogue of Gosper’s identity.

Gosper
Author of an identity for which a new q-analogue is presented alongside Yamaguchi's q-analogue.

Chu
Author of research contributions that inspired the work and the modified Abel lemma.

Bailey
Author of a classic text referred to for background on generalized hypergeometric series.

Abel Lemma
A lemma on summation by parts used as a basis for a modified technique.

Modified Abel Lemma
A variation of the Abel lemma on summation by parts, assumed to converge for the research.

Q-Analogue
A mathematical analogue to concepts such as factorials or identities discussed in the context of q-series.

Shifted Factorial
A construct defined for positive integers n and a variable x with (x)0 = 1.

Q-Shifted Factorial
A q-analogue of the shifted factorial defined for complex numbers a where |q| < 1.

Generalized Hypergeometric Series
Series defined with specific notation, referring to Bailey’s classic text.

Backward Difference Operator
An operator defined as nabla tau_n = tau_n - tau_n-1.

Forward Difference Operator
An operator defined as delta tau_n = tau_n - tau_n+1.

Gospers Identity
An identity for which a new q-analogue is presented, inequivalent to Yamaguchi's q-analogue.

Baileys Classic Text
A background text by Bailey referenced for theoretical context.

Section 1.1
The section titled Preliminaries covering definitions of factorials and series.

Section 2.1–2.3
Sections covering the broader construction of q-analogues applied in the context.

Sequence Tau N
A mathematical sequence $(\tau_n : n \in \mathbb{N}_0)$ used to define backward and forward difference operators.

Natural Numbers
The set $\mathbb{N}_0$ used as the domain for the sequence index.

Complex Number
The mathematical domain for the parameter $a$ in the definition of q-shifted factorials.

Summation Lemma
The lemma referenced as equation (3) used in the summation by parts.

Technique
A q-version technique applied to the construction of q-analogues based on a modified Abel lemma.

Yamaguchis Q-Analogue
A simplified proof is obtained for this q-analogue by Campbell and Cantarini.

Defines the generalized hypergeometric series $_rF_s$ as a power series with Pochhammer symbols. This fundamental special function generalizes Bessel, Legendre, and Gauss hypergeometric functions. In the surrounding q-calculus context, it represents the classical q=1 limit of basic hypergeometric series with q-shifted factorials. The series converges for $|x|<1$ when $r \\le s+1$ and connects differential equation solutions, orthogonal polynomials, and combinatorial mathematics through its analytic properties and parameter dependencies.

Defines the fundamental q-Pochhammer symbol used in q-series and basic hypergeometric function theory. This equation establishes the q-analogue of the classical shifted factorial, providing the building block for q-hypergeometric series, partition identities, and quantum calculus applications. The definitions for finite n and infinite products enable rigorous treatment of q-analogs of summations, identities, and transformation formulas essential to modern combinatorics and mathematical physics.

This equation defines the basic hypergeometric series of type $_r\phi_{r-1}$, a fundamental construct in q-calculus that generalizes classical hypergeometric functions through q-deformation. The series incorporates q-shifted factorials and converges under the condition |q| < 1. It serves as the foundational building block for q-analogue identities, connections to modified Abel lemmas, and applications in mathematical physics and combinatorics. The $_r\phi_{r-1}$ series links to classical analysis as a q→1 limit, providing bridge constructions between q-calculus frameworks and traditional special function theory.

Defines the fundamental coefficient term for basic hypergeometric series ($\_r\phi_s$) using $q$-shifted factorials and parameter ratios. This identity establishes the structural relationship between upper and lower series parameters, enabling the construction of $q$-analogues of classical hypergeometric series. It serves as a prerequisite for applying summation techniques, such as the modified Abel lemma, to derive complex identities like Gosper's $q$-analogue. The equation bridges classical hypergeometric theory and $q$-calculus, specifically facilitating the transition between $q$-deformed and classical factorials via limits, thereby underpinning the broader proof methodologies discussed in the text.

This equation defines a shorthand notation for the ratio of infinite $q$-Pochhammer symbols, essential for simplifying expressions in $q$-calculus. It establishes the algebraic structure underlying infinite basic hypergeometric series terms and Bailey pair coefficients, enabling efficient application of summation by parts and modified Abel lemmas cited in the context.

data.entity_name: Modified Abel Lemma on Summation by Parts (equation)
data.content: A discrete summation by parts identity essential for constructing q-analogues of classical combinatorial identities. The Modified Abel Lemma generalizes integration by parts to discrete sequences, providing a systematic method for deriving new q-analogue identities such as Gosper's identity. Key for q-calculus frameworks in combinatorial analysis with |q| < 1 convergence requirements.

data.entity_name: A FURTHER q-ANALOGUE OF GOSPER'S STRANGE SERIES (header)
data.content: This header introduces a specific section detailing a novel q-analogue derived for Gosper's Strange Series identity. It marks a transition from mathematical preliminaries to the main results, likely corresponding to the discussion of new identities derived via the modified Abel lemma. It signals the presentation of significant advancements in q-calculus, distinguishing this identity from Yamaguchi's analogue, and serves as a key reference point for researchers investigating Gosper identities and summation methods in q-series contexts.

This element marks the beginning of Section 2 in the mathematical document. Situated after the Preliminaries on the first page, it serves as a structural divider between introductory theory (Definitions 1, q-series) and the main analytical results. It explicitly signals the start of the discussions on constructing q-analogues using the modified Abel lemma for Sections 2.1–2.3, guiding the reader from background context to the primary contributions outlined in the paper.
